title: Temperature Sensor Threshold Alerts (Meraki MT)
dataSources: `index=meraki sourcetype=meraki:sensorreadingshistory metric="temperature"` from the `cisco_meraki_sensor_readings_history` modular input in `Splunk_TA_cisco_meraki` (Splunkbase 5580). Default poll: 86400s. Key fields: `serial`, `network.id`, `network.name`, `metric`, `temperature.celsius`, `temperature.fahrenheit`, `ts`.
spl:
index=meraki sourcetype="meraki:sensorreadingshistory" metric="temperature"
| rename "temperature.celsius" as temp_c
| stats latest(temp_c) as current_temp_c, min(temp_c) as min_temp_c, max(temp_c) as max_temp_c by serial, "network.name"
| where current_temp_c > 30 OR current_temp_c < 5
| sort - current_temp_c
description: Alerts when environmental temperatures exceed safe thresholds to prevent equipment damage.
value: Alerts when environmental temperatures exceed safe thresholds to prevent equipment damage.
implementation: Configure `cisco_meraki_sensor_readings_history` in `Splunk_TA_cisco_meraki` (Splunkbase 5580) to poll `GET /organizations/{orgId}/sensor/readings/history` for MT temperature readings. Filter on `metric="temperature"`. Alert when sensors report temperatures outside the safe range (5-30┬░C by default ŌĆö adjust per equipment class).
visualization: Temperature gauge per location; trend timeline; alert dashboard.
dataModelAcceleration: Not applicable (no CIM or Operational_Telemetry data model in this SPL).
knownFalsePositives: Temperature alarms during scheduled HVAC maintenance, controlled setback overrides, or sensor calibration.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
ŌĆó Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Cisco Meraki Add-on for Splunk` (Splunkbase 5580).
ŌĆó Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=meraki sourcetype=meraki:sensorreadingshistory metric="temperature"` from the `cisco_meraki_sensor_readings_history` modular input in `Splunk_TA_cisco_meraki` (Splunkbase 5580). Default poll: 86400s. Key fields: `serial`, `network.id`, `network.name`, `metric`, `temperature.celsius`, `temperature.fahrenheit`, `ts`..
ŌĆó For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ŌĆö Configure data collection
Configure `cisco_meraki_sensor_readings_history` in `Splunk_TA_cisco_meraki` (Splunkbase 5580) to poll `GET /organizations/{orgId}/sensor/readings/history` for MT temperature readings. Filter on `metric="temperature"`. Alert when sensors report temperatures outside the safe range (5-30┬░C by default ŌĆö adjust per equipment class).

Step 2 ŌĆö Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=meraki sourcetype="meraki:sensorreadingshistory" metric="temperature"
| rename "temperature.celsius" as temp_c
| stats latest(temp_c) as current_temp_c, min(temp_c) as min_temp_c, max(temp_c) as max_temp_c by serial, "network.name"
| where current_temp_c > 30 OR current_temp_c < 5
| sort - current_temp_c
```

Understanding this SPL

**Temperature Sensor Threshold Alerts (Meraki MT)** ŌĆö Alerts when environmental temperatures exceed safe thresholds to prevent equipment damage.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=meraki sourcetype=meraki:sensorreadingshistory metric="temperature"` from the `cisco_meraki_sensor_readings_history` modular input in `Splunk_TA_cisco_meraki` (Splunkbase 5580). Default poll: 86400s. Key fields: `serial`, `network.id`, `network.name`, `metric`, `temperature.celsius`, `temperature.fahrenheit`, `ts`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Cisco Meraki Add-on for Splunk` (Splunkbase 5580). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedŌĆörename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: meraki; **sourcetype**: meraki:sensorreadingshistory. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

ŌĆó Scopes the data: index=meraki, sourcetype="meraki:sensorreadingshistory". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
ŌĆó Renames fields with `rename` for clarity or joins.
ŌĆó `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by serial, "network.name"** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
ŌĆó Filters the current rows with `where current_temp_c > 30 OR current_temp_c < 5` ŌĆö typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
ŌĆó Orders rows with `sort` ŌĆö combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 ŌĆö Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ŌĆö Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Temperature gauge per location; trend timeline; alert dashboard.
grandmaExplanation: We watch how your building systems behave. We help you know if something is off with heating, cooling, and room comfort before harm, damage, or wasted effort piles up.
